summary: DHH argues that wisdom is not about accumulating knowledge but about repeatedly practicing and internalizing lessons until they become instinctive. He reflects on how he once dismissed rereading books as wasteful, only to realize that repetition is essential for wisdom to stick. Drawing on Stoic philosophy, particularly Epictetus, he compares wisdom to physical exercise — knowing how to do a push-up is worthless without actually doing the reps. He extends this to his own habit of repeating the same ideas in writing and interviews, framing repetition not as redundancy but as necessary practice.
key_points: Wisdom is useless unless it can be recalled and applied when needed — knowledge alone has no value
Repetition is the mechanism by which wisdom becomes internalized, analogous to physical exercise
Stoic philosophy provides a practical 'exercise program' for wisdom, especially the ideas of imagined suffering and focusing only on what you can control
Repeating yourself in writing or speaking is not redundancy but a form of cognitive training for both speaker and audience
Reading foundational texts repeatedly (like Epictetus's Manual) keeps hard-won insights accessible in moments of need
money_quotes: The hallmark of great wisdom is not what you know, but what you know and can put to use.
The globe is full of learned idiots, unable or incapable of following the wisdom they have accumulated.
There's no prize for a closet full of axioms or insights, if you leave it all in there, and venture philosophically naked into the world.
You don't get in shape by knowing how to do a push-up but by doing a hundred a week. Accept that wisdom is a form of mental exercise.
As soon as you actually need this pithy little reminder, it's usually nowhere to be found. To have it emerge top of mind in a time of need requires immense training.
You are what you think. Become better by thinking better. Think better by repeating the few hard lessons you know you need the most.
key_insight: Wisdom is not an intellectual achievement but a physical discipline — it requires constant repetition to be available when you actually need it.
